text: Hi everyone. Although sometimes used interchangeably, the words sigmoid colectomy and high anterior resection points toward different parts of the left colon. Today's video presentation is a collage of three sigmoid colectomies stroke high anterior resections to take you through this procedure step by step.
The patient is positioned in a steep head-down position to facilitate laparoscopic access to the pelvic region, with ports placed according to standard ergonomic practices for efficient surgery.

The surgeon tilts the operating table side to side, gathering the small bowel to one side of the abdomen, providing a clear view of the aorta and duodenum as landmarks for starting the dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery and vein.

The assistant pulls the sigmoid colon upward, revealing the inferior mesenteric artery. The surgeon begins the dissection by splitting the peritoneum over the sacral promontory, allowing CO₂ to fill the retroperitoneal space. The dissection proceeds down to the upper part of the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane.

The surgeon continues splitting the peritoneum between the bifurcation of the common iliac vessels, progressing toward the root of the inferior mesenteric artery. Care is taken to avoid the superior hypogastric plexus visible in this area.

It's not always essential to take the dissection right down to the root of the IMA as literature is divided regarding the high or low dissection of this artery. Nonetheless, lymph node yields are shown to be consistently high if arteries are taken at their origin, especially for cancer surgery.
The surgeon exposes the ureter, which is visible beneath a thin layer of fascia, indicating that the dissection is proceeding in the correct tissue plane.
The surgeon carefully proceeds with the dissection in an elderly obese patient, navigating through friable fat tissue to minimize bleeding risk.

The surgeon continues the dissection from the sacral promontory towards the top of the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The correct plane is identified by the presence of loose areolar tissue. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is observed to be short, stubby, and surrounded by fat.

The surgeon attempts to skeletonize the vessel, preparing it for division. If the vessel remains bulky, a vascular stapler is used for division instead of clips. The procedure progresses with a medial to lateral dissection after addressing the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and sometimes the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV).

The surgeon joins the medial to lateral dissection with the top of the TME plane dissection by taking down adhesions, facilitating the operation. The mesocolon is separated from the retroperitoneal structures, ensuring to stay in the correct plane, which is visually higher on the mesocolon.

The surgeon identifies the source muscle and tendon, indicating entry into the wrong plane. Adjusting the grasper and holding the peritoneum on the right side reveals the correct plane, which is slightly higher. The superior hypogastric plexus and the right and left hypogastric nerves are clearly visible at the top of the TME plane during the high anterior section.

The surgeon performs a lateral to medial dissection, opening the peritoneum to reveal the pedicle package at the internal border of the mesocolon. The dissection progresses rapidly as air from the opposite side becomes visible.

The surgeon turns the dissection toward the pelvic region, focusing on the lower sigmoid colon and upper rectum. The previously completed medial to lateral dissection facilitates the opening of the peritoneum in this area. The left ureter is carefully protected throughout the procedure.

The surgeon maintains dissection within the TME plane, indicated by the presence of loose areolar tissue. The team prepares the distal and proximal parts of the bowel for anastomosis. The assistant holds the proximal left colon at the proposed line of resection, highlighting where to divide the mesocolon.

The surgeon mobilizes the left bowel, ensuring the proximal bowel can reach into the pelvis for anastomosis. The mesorectum is divided, with the surgeon performing lateral and posterior division from the right side.

The surgeon divides the mesorectum during a high anterior resection, noting the potential for bleeding in this vascular area. The rectum is dissected using a stapler, and attention is given to the long rectal stump during the insertion of the stapling device from below.

For this procedure, we always do a leak test by filling the peritoneum with water and inserting some air with a rigid sigmoidoscope. Please post your comments in the section below. Like and subscribe for further such videos. Thank you for watching.
